linked_sections.heading: Model setup and initialization
linked_sections.text_snippet: First, we evaluated the effect of initialization of the model's weights for the classification of T2D images versus non-T2D images. We compared five different strategies: (1) random initialization; (2) ImageNet weights; (3) model pretrained on global retinal microvascular measurements (T2D biomarkers), including vessel caliber and vessel tortuosity; 12 (4) A multi-target learning (MTL) approach with random initialization and (5) Multi-target learning with ImageNet weights.

linked_sections.paper_title: Explainable Fundus Image Curation and Lesion Detection in Diabetic Retinopathy
linked_sections.heading: CONCLUSION
linked_sections.text_snippet: We presented a framework for curating fundus imaging data by jointly considering overall image quality and the consistency of lesion annotations, with the aim of producing reliable training corpora for AI-based detection of biomarkers associated with the presence and severity of diabetic retinopathy. The proposed pipeline integrates an explainable image-quality classifier with deep learning-driven lesion suggestions and visualization enhancement (CLAHE, gamma-based illumination correction), complemented by lesion-specific post-processing.

papers.title: Medical Knowledge-Guided Deep Curriculum Learning for Elbow Fracture Diagnosis from X-Ray Images
papers.abstract: Elbow fractures are one of the most common fracture types. Diagnoses on elbow fractures often need the help of radiographic imaging to be read and analyzed by a specialized radiologist with years of training. Thanks to the recent advances of deep learning, a model that can classify and detect different types of bone fractures needs only hours of training and has shown promising results. However, most existing deep learning models are purely data-driven, lacking incorporation of known domain knowledge from human experts. In this work, we propose a novel deep learning method to diagnose elbow fracture from elbow X-ray images by integrating domain-specific medical knowledge into a curriculum learning framework. In our method, the training data are permutated by sampling without replacement at the beginning of each training epoch. The sampling probability of each training sample is guided by a scoring criterion constructed based on clinically known knowledge from human experts, where the scoring indicates the diagnosis difficultness of different elbow fracture subtypes. We also propose an algorithm that updates the sampling probabilities at each epoch, which is applicable to other sampling-based curriculum learning frameworks. We design an experiment with 1865 elbow X-ray images for a fracture/normal binary classification task and compare our proposed method to a baseline method and a previous method using multiple metrics. Our results show that the proposed method achieves the highest classification performance. Also, our proposed probability update algorithm boosts the performance of the previous method.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on diabetic retinopathy grading from fundus photographs, a specific medical imaging task. The paper addresses elbow fracture diagnosis from X-ray images, which is also a medical imaging classification problem but for a different anatomical region and pathology. However, the user's idea specifies a transfer learning approach with ResNet-50 fine-tuned for diabetic retinopathy grading, while the paper proposes a medical knowledge-guided deep curriculum learning method using a VGG16 backbone for elbow fracture diagnosis from X-ray images. These represent different deep learning architectures, training paradigms (transfer learning vs. curriculum learning with knowledge integration), and medical applications. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper are in the medical imaging domain, specifically using deep learning for diagnosis from image data. However, the user's idea focuses on diabetic retinopathy grading from fundus photographs, while the paper addresses elbow fracture diagnosis from X-ray images. This represents the same discipline (medical imaging/AI in healthcare) but different sub-areas and application targets (retinopathy vs. fractures, fundus photos vs. X-rays). | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes a transfer learning approach using ResNet-50 for diabetic retinopathy grading. In contrast, the paper focuses on a medical knowledge-guided deep curriculum learning method for elbow fracture diagnosis from X-ray images, using a VGG16 backbone. The core contributions, problem domains, and methodological innovations are distinct.

papers.title: Structure-preserving Guided Retinal Image Filtering and Its Application for Optic Disc Analysis
papers.abstract: Retinal fundus photographs have been used in the diagnosis of many ocular diseases such as glaucoma, pathological myopia, age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy. With the development of computer science, computer aided diagnosis has been developed to process and analyse the retinal images automatically. One of the challenges in the analysis is that the quality of the retinal image is often degraded. For example, a cataract in human lens will attenuate the retinal image, just as a cloudy camera lens which reduces the quality of a photograph. It often obscures the details in the retinal images and posts challenges in retinal image processing and analysing tasks. In this paper, we approximate the degradation of the retinal images as a combination of human-lens attenuation and scattering. A novel structure-preserving guided retinal image filtering (SGRIF) is then proposed to restore images based on the attenuation and scattering model. The proposed SGRIF consists of a step of global structure transferring and a step of global edge-preserving smoothing. Our results show that the proposed SGRIF method is able to improve the contrast of retinal images, measured by histogram flatness measure, histogram spread and variability of local luminosity. In addition, we further explored the benefits of SGRIF for subsequent retinal image processing and analysing tasks. In the two applications of deep learning based optic cup segmentation and sparse learning based cup-to-disc ratio (CDR) computation, our results show that we are able to achieve more accurate optic cup segmentation and CDR measurements from images processed by SGRIF.

papers.title: Direct Classification of Type 2 Diabetes From Retinal Fundus Images in a Population-based Sample From The Maastricht Study
papers.abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is a chronic metabolic disorder that can lead to blindness and cardiovascular disease. Information about early stage T2D might be present in retinal fundus images, but to what extent these images can be used for a screening setting is still unknown. In this study, deep neural networks were employed to differentiate between fundus images from individuals with and without T2D. We investigated three methods to achieve high classification performance, measured by the area under the receiver operating curve (ROC-AUC). A multi-target learning approach to simultaneously output retinal biomarkers as well as T2D works best (AUC = 0.746 [$\pm$0.001]). Furthermore, the classification performance can be improved when images with high prediction uncertainty are referred to a specialist. We also show that the combination of images of the left and right eye per individual can further improve the classification performance (AUC = 0.758 [$\pm$0.003]), using a simple averaging approach. The results are promising, suggesting the feasibility of screening for T2D from retinal fundus images.

While both use retinal fundus images and deep learning, the specific problem being addressed (grading DR vs. detecting T2D) is different. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper utilize deep learning with transfer learning from ImageNet. The paper explicitly mentions evaluating 'ImageNet weights' as an initialization strategy. papers.title: Explainable Fundus Image Curation and Lesion Detection in Diabetic Retinopathy
papers.abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) affects individuals with long-term diabetes. Without early diagnosis, DR can lead to vision loss. Fundus photography captures the structure of the retina along with abnormalities indicative of the stage of the disease. Artificial Intelligence (AI) can support clinicians in identifying these lesions, reducing manual workload, but models require high-quality annotated datasets. Due to the complexity of retinal structures, errors in image acquisition and lesion interpretation of manual annotators can occur. We proposed a quality-control framework, ensuring only high-standard data is used for evaluation and AI training. First, an explainable feature-based classifier is used to filter inadequate images. The features are extracted both using image processing and contrastive learning. Then, the images are enhanced and put subject to annotation, using deep-learning-based assistance. Lastly, the agreement between annotators calculated using derived formulas determines the usability of the annotations.

Although both relate to AI in DR, the specific problem of data curation and quality control is distinct from directly grading the severity of DR. | Method: The user's idea proposes a transfer learning approach using ResNet-50 for diabetic retinopathy grading. The paper, while mentioning 'deep-learning-based assistance' and 'deep learning-driven lesion suggestions' for annotation, focuses on a quality-control framework for curating fundus imaging data, using an 'explainable feature-based classifier' and 'contrastive learning' for image filtering. The paper, however, focuses on a quality-control framework for curating fundus imaging data, including an explainable image-quality classifier and deep learning-driven lesion suggestions for annotation assistance. While both relate to diabetic retinopathy and deep learning, their core contributions and methodologies are distinct.

papers.title: Automatic diagnosis of retinal diseases from color retinal images
papers.abstract: Teleophthalmology holds a great potential to improve the quality, access, and affordability in health care. For patients, it can reduce the need for travel and provide the access to a superspecialist. Ophthalmology lends itself easily to telemedicine as it is a largely image based diagnosis. The main goal of the proposed system is to diagnose the type of disease in the retina and to automatically detect and segment retinal diseases without human supervision or interaction. The proposed system will diagnose the disease present in the retina using a neural network based classifier.The extent of the disease spread in the retina can be identified by extracting the textural features of the retina. This system will diagnose the following type of diseases: Diabetic Retinopathy and Drusen.

existing_examples.name: Deep Learning for Diabetic Retinopathy Grading (General Research Area)
existing_examples.description: Numerous research papers and projects have explored deep learning, including transfer learning with ResNet-50, for DR grading. This is a very active and well-established research field.

existing_examples.name: Google's AI for Diabetic Retinopathy
existing_examples.description: Google has published extensively on using deep learning for DR detection and grading, including clinical validation, often using architectures similar to ResNet and training on large datasets like EyePACS.

existing_examples.name: Various academic papers (e.g., Gulshan et al. 2016, Quellec et al. 2017, etc.)
existing_examples.description: Many papers specifically detail using CNNs (including ResNet variants), transfer learning from ImageNet, and evaluation on EyePACS and Messidor-2 for DR grading. This exact methodology is a standard approach in the field.

existing_examples.name: Kaggle Diabetic Retinopathy Detection Competition (2015)
existing_examples.description: This competition significantly popularized the use of deep learning on fundus images for DR, leading to many solutions that employed similar transfer learning techniques.

reality_check.assessment: The proposed idea describes a very common and well-established methodology in the field of medical image analysis, specifically for diabetic retinopathy grading. Using ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuning, and evaluating on EyePACS and Messidor-2 are standard practices that have been extensively explored and published in numerous research papers and even commercial applications (e.g., Google's efforts). This is not a novel research idea in its current form.
reality_check.recommendation: While the methodology is sound, it is not novel. To make this a publishable research idea, you would need to introduce a significant innovation. This could include: a novel network architecture, a new transfer learning strategy, a unique data augmentation technique, a method to address specific challenges (e.g., imbalanced datasets, low-quality images), a multi-modal approach (combining with OCT, patient data), or a new interpretation of results/explainability. Simply applying a standard technique to standard datasets will not be considered original research.

matched_sections.paper_title: Direct Classification of Type 2 Diabetes From Retinal Fundus Images in a Population-based Sample From The Maastricht Study
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Retinal fundus imaging is widely used for the detection of diabetic retinopathy (DR), one of the complications of T2D. Over the last few years, deep learning (DL) has been proposed for automated analysis of retinal fundus images.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper discuss the use of deep learning for analyzing retinal fundus images related to diabetes. The user focuses on 'diabetic retinopathy grading', while the paper focuses on 'T2D detection', which are related but distinct problems.

matched_sections.paper_title: Direct Classification of Type 2 Diabetes From Retinal Fundus Images in a Population-based Sample From The Maastricht Study
matched_sections.heading: Model setup and initialization
matched_sections.text_snippet: First, we evaluated the effect of initialization of the model's weights for the classification of T2D images versus non-T2D images. We compared five different strategies: (1) random initialization; (2) ImageNet weights; (3) model pretrained on global retinal microvascular measurements (T2D biomarkers), including vessel caliber and vessel tortuosity; 12 (4) A multi-target learning (MTL) approach with random initialization and (5) Multi-target learning with ImageNet weights.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'transfer learning approach using a ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet', which directly overlaps with the paper's mention of using 'ImageNet weights' as an initialization strategy for their deep learning model, indicating a similar transfer learning methodology.

matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is a chronic metabolic disorder that can lead to blindness and cardiovascular disease. matched_sections.paper_title: Explainable Fundus Image Curation and Lesion Detection in Diabetic Retinopathy
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) affects individuals with long-term diabetes. Without early diagnosis, DR can lead to vision loss. Fundus photography captures the structure of the retina along with abnormalities indicative of the stage of the disease. Artificial Intelligence (AI) can support clinicians in identifying these lesions, reducing manual workload, but models require high-quality annotated datasets.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address the problem of using AI for diabetic retinopathy, specifically involving fundus photography. The user's idea focuses on grading, while the paper focuses on lesion detection and data curation for AI training.

matched_sections.paper_title: Explainable Fundus Image Curation and Lesion Detection in Diabetic Retinopathy
matched_sections.heading: RELATED WORK
matched_sections.text_snippet: Yi et al. have relied on Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) model with zero-shot transfer learning. For assessing the quality of medical images, antonym prompts ('Good photo' versus 'Bad photo', 'Bright photo' versus 'Dark photo') were considered. To tackle the challenge that CLIP works with fixed-sized inputs, positional embeddings of the Transformer model are replaced with ResNet.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'transfer learning' and 'ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet'. While the paper's main method is different (quality control framework), its 'RELATED WORK' section discusses a method by Yi et al. that uses 'zero-shot transfer learning' and replaces Transformer positional embeddings with 'ResNet' for medical image quality assessment. This indicates a shared methodological concept of using ResNet and transfer learning in the broader context of medical image analysis, although for a different specific task (quality assessment vs. grading).

matched_sections.paper_title: Explainable Fundus Image Curation and Lesion Detection in Diabetic Retinopathy
matched_sections.heading: CONCLUSION
matched_sections.text_snippet: We presented a framework for curating fundus imaging data by jointly considering overall image quality and the consistency of lesion annotations, with the aim of producing reliable training corpora for AI-based detection of biomarkers associated with the presence and severity of diabetic retinopathy. The proposed pipeline integrates an explainable image-quality classifier with deep learning-driven lesion suggestions and visualization enhancement (CLAHE, gamma-based illumination correction), complemented by lesion-specific post-processing.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes a specific application of transfer learning for 'diabetic retinopathy grading'. The paper's contribution is a 'framework for curating fundus imaging data' for 'AI-based detection of biomarkers associated with the presence and severity of diabetic retinopathy'. While the paper focuses on data curation and lesion detection, the underlying goal of supporting AI for DR severity assessment (which grading implies) shows a partial overlap in the ultimate contribution towards improving DR diagnosis.
